title: Lesson Quiz: Citizenship and Civic Responsibility
description: Assessment of the concept that citizenship includes rights and responsibilities, and civic participation supports society.

choices.text: Avoiding all public responsibilities
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Citizenship includes responsibilities, not avoiding them.

choices.text: Being a member of a country with rights and responsibilities
choices.feedback: Correct. This is the lesson definition of citizenship.

choices.text: Having power over all laws
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Citizenship does not mean controlling all laws.

choices.text: Only voting in elections
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Voting is one example of participation, not the full definition of citizenship.

choices.text: Duties citizens must fulfill
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Duties describe responsibilities, not rights.

choices.text: Freedoms and protections given to citizens
choices.feedback: Correct. Rights are freedoms and protections.

choices.text: Penalties for not following laws
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Penalties are not rights.

choices.text: Types of government authority
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Rights are not forms of authority.

choices.text: Optional actions citizens never need to consider
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Responsibilities are expected duties, not optional ideas.

choices.text: Freedoms protected by law
choices.feedback: Incorrect. That describes rights, not responsibilities.

choices.text: Duties citizens are expected to fulfill
choices.feedback: Correct. Responsibilities are duties such as following laws and contributing to society.

choices.text: Government services provided by cities and states
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Government services are not the same as citizen responsibilities.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows civic participation?

choices.text: Ignoring elections and community issues
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Ignoring public issues is not civic participation.

choices.text: Voting in an election and staying informed
choices.feedback: Correct. Voting and staying informed are clear examples of civic participation.

choices.text: Avoiding all laws that seem inconvenient
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Avoiding laws is not responsible civic behavior.

choices.text: Letting others make every public decision
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Civic participation means being involved, not withdrawing completely.

choices.text: It helps society function and supports the common good
choices.feedback: Correct. The lesson explains that civic responsibility helps society function effectively.

choices.text: It removes the need for rights
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Civic responsibility does not replace rights.

choices.text: It only matters during elections
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Civic responsibility includes many actions beyond elections.

choices.text: It allows citizens to ignore community needs
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Civic responsibility involves supporting, not ignoring, community needs.
